text: Robotic approach in morbidly obese patients with low rectal cancer may confer advantages over laparoscopic approach. This is a fifty-year-old male patient, diagnosed case of stage III low rectal cancer, received neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy, underwent surgery ten weeks post-treatment. His BMI was forty.

He had a palpable rectal mass five centimeters from anal verge located posteriorly. He had an endoscopy done, which showed a lesion, as shown in the picture. His biopsy showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. This patient showed good response to the chemoradiotherapy and had a palpable ridge at the previous site of tumor. We have pre-treatment MRI image as shown in the picture. We have post-chemoradiotherapy MRI image. CT staging revealed no metastasis. MDT decision was to proceed with surgery.

For this procedure, robotic XI system with integrated table motion was used. This diagram shows the standard port placement for all robotic TME procedures performed in our institution. Sometime with a B's patient, an additional five millimeter port for assistant left hand is placed between R one and R three as shown. This help facilitate exposing the small bowel to push along the gauze, giving robotic operating console surgeon exposure to the medial side of the mesentery to undertake the dissection.

The surgeon positions the patient with a head-down tilt of twenty-two degrees and a right-side tilt of seven to nine degrees to expose the medial side of the mesentery. The greater omentum is placed above the transverse colon, and the pelvis is cleared of small bowel, which is moved medially using small gauze. The dissection begins at the sacral promontory, following a medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery at its root and secures it using a white vascular stapler. The dissection proceeds cranially in a medial to lateral approach, staying anterior to Girota's fascia while protecting the ureter and gonadal vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein is then identified, isolated, and clipped just below the lower border of the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the pancreas as a landmark to access the lesser sac, facilitating the mobilization of the splenic flexure. Monopolar diathermy is used for lateral mobilization, cutting along the white line, while gauze is employed to prevent small bowel injury. The splenic flexure is routinely mobilized in all total mesorectal excisions (TMEs).

The surgeon develops the plane above the colon, staying close to the transverse colon and separating the greater omentum in the embryological plane using monopolar diathermy. The patient is positioned head up to facilitate access to the supracolic compartment and open the lesser sac.

The video shows a clear view of the pancreas as the surgeon dissects the mesotransverse colon at the level of the pancreas to minimize the risk of marginal artery injury. The splenic flexure is completely released.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by dissecting between the hypogastric nerves at the back of the superior rectal artery, using gentle traction and monopolar diathermy with paintbrush-like movements. An assistant uses suction to minimize smoke from the diathermy. The dissection stays at the top of the white layers, referred to as the yellow side of the white, to facilitate deeper dissection. The focus then shifts to the right lateral side, staying behind the seminal vesicle, and involves cutting the cuff of peritoneum held by the robotic left hand for anterior dissection. The operation progresses to the left of the rectum following initial anterior marking.
The surgeon develops a plane either outside or underneath the Denonvilliers fascia, focusing anteriorly due to the posterior location of the tumor.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the left hypogastric nerve plexus while continuing dissection from the left pararectal space to connect with the previously established plane below the seminal vesicle. The dissection progresses anally, staying under the seminal vesicle and behind the Denonvilliers fascia to minimize the risk of damaging the hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon approaches the pelvic floor on the right side, joining the previously created planes both anteriorly and posteriorly. The dissection is performed with repeated movements and clear exposure of the surgical field. The same technique is then applied to the left side of the specimen.

The surgeon releases the anococcygeal ligament from the lower third of the rectum, preparing for stapling. A flexible sigmoidoscopy is performed, followed by the introduction of a stapling device through the twelve millimeter arm number three. The surgeon aims to divide the lower rectum from twelve to six o'clock in a posterior direction.

The surgeon uses a stapling device with a green cartridge to perform two staple firings on the completely mobilized rectal tube. The first staple line is applied, followed by a second staple line using the same curvature, aiming for posterior cuts. The robotic camera operator ensures the staple line extends beyond the lower end of the rectal tube.

The surgeon performs a CDH-XXIX anastomosis at the coloanal junction, followed by the creation of a covering loop ileostomy for protection. The removed specimen reveals a complete total mesorectal excision (TME) with grade III classification, showing no residual tumor and two positive lymph nodes out of fourteen, indicating stage III YPT-Zero N-I-B M-Zero.
